messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Generative AI for Statistical Science (Free Seminar) – last chance
messages.subject_clean: Generative AI for Statistical Science (Free Seminar) – last chance
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance to join our 1-day seminar Generative AI for Statistical Science (Free Seminar) livestreaming March 3 with Lei Ding (Department of Statistics, University of Manitoba). This seminar introduces modern generative AI paradigms, offering powerful statistical tools for modeling complex data structures. Ecologists and environmental scientists can leverage these methods for tasks such as generating synthetic environmental datasets to explore hypothetical scenarios, imputing missing values in long-term monitoring data, or performing advanced text analysis on ecological literature and field observations. Understanding these techniques will enable researchers to enhance data analysis capabilities, address common challenges in ecological data management, and explore novel approaches to environmental modeling within their R-based workflows.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/generative-ai-for-statistical-science-fr) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities using Vegan
messages.subject_clean: Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities using Vegan
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to analyse complex ecological community data with our live online
course *Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities using Vegan
(VGNR09)*.

https://prstats.org/course/multivariate-analysis-of-ecological-communities-using-vegan-vgnr09/

This intensive training provides a practical introduction to multivariate
statistical methods used to explore, visualise, and interpret ecological
community datasets. Using the widely used R package *vegan*, participants
will work with real data to understand patterns in species assemblages and
environmental gradients. ()

The course covers:

   -

   Exploring and visualising multivariate ecological datasets
   -

   Ordination methods such as PCA, NMDS, CA and RDA
   -

   Clustering and similarity analyses
   -

   Hypothesis testing using methods such as PERMANOVA
   -

   Interpreting multivariate outputs in ecological contexts

The training is delivered live online, with recordings available
afterwards. Participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 20?24 April 2026
Duration: 5 days (approximately 7 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?485

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, ecological
consultants, and environmental professionals working with community or
biodiversity datasets who want practical, reproducible workflows for
analysing species assemblages in R.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/multivariate-analysis-of-ecological-communities-using-vegan-vgnr09/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Deep Learning Using R
messages.subject_clean: Deep Learning Using R
messages.body_plain:
Build practical deep learning skills in R with our live online course *Deep
Learning Using R (DLUR01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/deep-learning-using-r-dlur01/

This hands-on workshop introduces modern deep learning techniques using the
R *torch* ecosystem. Participants will learn both the core theory behind
neural networks and how to implement deep learning models through guided
coding exercises suitable for real research workflows.

The course covers:

   -

   Foundations of artificial neural networks and deep learning
   -

   Building multilayer perceptrons in R
   -

   Convolutional neural networks for image data
   -

   Transformer architectures and modern deep learning workflows
   -

   Practical model training, evaluation, and interpretation in R ()

The training is delivered live online, with recordings available
afterwards. Participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 21?22 April 2026
Duration: 2 days (approximately 6 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?300

This course is suitable for researchers, postgraduate students, and
analysts who are comfortable with R and want a practical introduction to
neural networks, CNNs, and transformer models for scientific data analysis.

https://prstats.org/course/deep-learning-using-r-dlur01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Standard Modelling Procedure for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling
messages.subject_clean: Standard Modelling Procedure for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to build robust, reproducible species distribution and ecological
niche models in our live online course *Standard Modelling Procedure for
Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling (SDMS01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/standard-modelling-procedure-for-species-distribution-and-ecological-niche-modelling-sdms01/

This applied R training introduces a structured workflow for building,
evaluating, and interpreting species distribution models (SDMs) and
ecological niche models (ENMs), widely used in ecology, conservation
science, and biodiversity research.

Participants will work through practical examples and learn how to apply
best-practice modelling procedures to real ecological datasets.

The course covers:

   -

   Core concepts behind species distribution and ecological niche modelling
   -

   Preparing species occurrence and environmental predictor data
   -

   Model calibration, validation, and interpretation
   -

   Avoiding common modelling pitfalls and biases
   -

   Reproducible modelling workflows in R

The training is delivered live online, with recordings available
afterwards. Participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 23?24 April 2026
Duration: 2 days (approximately 5 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?275

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, ecological
consultants, and environmental professionals who want a practical
introduction to SDMs and ENMs using standardised workflows in R.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/standard-modelling-procedure-for-species-distribution-and-ecological-niche-modelling-sdms01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Help needed: unmarkedMultFrame dimension mismatch in yearlySiteCovs
messages.subject_clean: Help needed: unmarkedMultFrame dimension mismatch in yearlySiteCovs
messages.body_plain:
Dear R-sig-ecology community,

I am currently using the unmarked package to create a unmarkedMultFrame for
analyzing wildlife data. I am facing an issue where the yearlySiteCovs
object, which I am passing as one of the arguments, contains elements that
appear as "arrays" rather than matrices, despite my efforts to convert them.

Here's the context:
yearlySiteCovs contains three periods (period1, period2, period3), each
having dimensions 368x18.
However, when I check the class of each element in yearlySiteCovs, I see
that they are reported as "arrays" instead of matrices.
Even after using functions like as.matrix() and drop(), the elements are
still identified as arrays, which is causing issues when I try to run the
unmarkedMultFrame model.

Example script:
# My yearlySiteCovs is a list containing three periods
yearlySiteCovs <- list(
  period1 = matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 368, ncol = 18),
  period2 = matrix(c(2, 3, 4, 5), nrow = 368, ncol = 18),
  period3 = matrix(c(3, 4, 5, 6), nrow = 368, ncol = 18)
)
# Attempt to convert to matrix
yearlySiteCovs <- lapply(yearlySiteCovs, function(x) {
  if (is.array(x)) {
    x <- as.vector(x)  # Flatten the array
    x <- matrix(x, nrow = 368, ncol = 18, byrow = TRUE)
  }
  return(x)
})

sapply(yearlySiteCovs, function(x) c(class(x), dim(x)))
    period1  period2  period3
[1,] "matrix" "matrix" "matrix"
*[2,] "array"  "array"  "array"*
[3,] "368"    "368"    "368"
[4,] "18"     "18"     "18"

Despite these efforts, the issue persists, and I receive the error:

"*At least one element of yearlySiteCovs has an incorrect number of
dimensions.*"

What I've tried:
I have used as.matrix(), drop(), and other standard methods to flatten and
convert the arrays into matrices.
I verified the dimensions of all the objects in yearlySiteCovs, and they
all appear to have the correct dimensions (368 rows and 18 columns).
I confirmed the format of y_matrix_processed, siteCovs, and obs_covs, all
of which are correctly formatted.
Could anyone help me understand why the "array" issue persists, and how I
can resolve it to ensure that yearlySiteCovs works correctly with
unmarkedMultFrame?

Any insights or suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Thank you in advance!

-- 

*Mohamed EL Mekkaoui*
*Doctorant - Chercheur* *Laboratoire de Biodiversit?, ?cologie et G?nome (*
BGCG*)* Facult? des Sciences, Universit? Mohammed V de Rabat

*LinkedIn :* <http://goog_85098632>linkedin.com/in/mohamed-el-mekkaoui

*Email :* mohamedelmekkaoui98 at gmail.com

*T?l :* +212 7 08 00 50 71

--

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] online course: Network Analysis in R,
messages.subject_clean: online course: Network Analysis in R,
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We are excited to announce our upcoming online course: Network Analysis in R, taking place from 4?7 May.
 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/network-analysis-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/network-analysis-in-r/ ) 
 
This hands-on course will teach you how to analyse, model, and visualize network data in R, with practical exercises and live coding sessions every day. You will learn to explore network structures, calculate network statistics, detect clusters, perform statistical modeling, and create clear, informative visualizations. While the focus is on life sciences, the skills are fully transferable to other fields.
 
You may also be interested in our companion course Network Analysis in Systems Biology with R/Bioconductor, which links networks with multi-omics data and biological function.
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/network-in-systems-biology/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/network-in-systems-biology/ ) 
 
 
 
Best regards, Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

[ Bluesky ]( https://bsky.app/profile/physaliacourses.bsky.social ) [ Linkedin ]( https://www.linkedin.com/in/physalia-courses-a64418127/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Network Analysis for Ecologists - Live online and on demand options
messages.subject_clean: Network Analysis for Ecologists - Live online and on demand options
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to analyse ecological networks with our course *Network Analysis
for Ecologists (NWAE02 / NWAEPR)*, available in both live online and
recorded formats.

Live course:
https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwae02/
Recorded course:
https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwaepr/

This applied R training introduces network analysis methods used to study
ecological interactions such as food webs, species co-occurrence, movement
networks, host?parasite systems, and social or behavioural networks.
Participants learn practical workflows for constructing, analysing, and
interpreting ecological networks using real datasets.

The course covers:

   -

   Concepts and terminology in ecological network analysis
   -

   Building and visualising ecological networks
   -

   Network metrics and interpretation
   -

   Community detection and modularity
   -

   Statistical approaches to testing ecological network hypotheses
   -

   Reproducible workflows in R

*Two flexible learning options*

*Live Online Course*

*Course details*
Dates: 3-4 November 2026
Duration: 2 days (approximately 3 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?250

   -

   Real-time interaction with the instructor
   -

   Opportunity to ask questions and discuss your own data
   -

   Structured schedule to stay on track
   -

   Networking with other participants

*Recorded Course*

*Course details*
Dates: Learn at your own pace
Duration: Approximately 12 hours
Format: Recorded with email/zoom support for questions
Fee: ?250

   -

   Learn at your own pace and revisit material anytime
   -

   Ideal for different time zones or busy schedules
   -

   Pause, replay, and review complex topics
   -

   Permanent access to lectures and materials

Both options include full course materials, datasets, and post-course
support.

*Who is this course for?*
Postgraduate students, researchers, and professionals working with
ecological interactions, biodiversity data, food webs, social networks,
movement connectivity, or species co-occurrence datasets.

Full details and registration:
Live course:
https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwae02/
Recorded course:
https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwaepr/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER & MixSIAR (SIMM12)
messages.subject_clean: Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER & MixSIAR (SIMM12)
messages.body_plain:
Join our live online course *Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER &
MixSIAR (SIMM12)* and gain practical skills in applying stable isotope
mixing models in R.

https://prstats.org/course/stable-isotope-mixing-models-using-siber-siar-mixsiar-simm12/

Instructors - Prof. Andrew Parnell and Prof. Andrew Jackson, authors of the
SIBER package - who better to learn from than the package authors
themselves!

This year we have a guest speaker, Dr Bernd Freymann, Director of ?
https://isolife.nl/ ? He will talk about isolife and their work. Isolife
develop, produce and sell depleted, partially, and uniformly 13C-labelled
(0.1 to >97 atom %13C) compounds derived from food plants, trees, herbs,
algae, and fungi. These intrinsically labelled compounds are used as Stable
Isotope Tracers and Internal Standards in research and diagnostics in the
Life Sciences. Isolife delivers their products to private companies and
public research organisations worldwide.

*More about the course* - This hands-on training provides a structured
introduction to the theory and implementation of mixing models used to
quantify source contributions in ecological and biogeochemical studies.
Participants will work through practical examples using widely adopted R
packages, learning how to fit, compare, and interpret models appropriately.

The course covers:

   -

   Foundations of stable isotope ecology and mixing model theory
   -

   Preparing and structuring isotope datasets
   -

   Implementing models in SIBER, SIAR, and MixSIAR
   -

   Understanding assumptions, uncertainty, and diagnostics
   -

   Interpreting outputs for ecological research

Delivered live online, this course allows for real-time interaction with
the instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of your own
datasets. All participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 27-30 April 2026
Duration: 4 days (approximately 7 hours per day)
Format: Live online (with recorded access afterward)
Fee: ?480

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, and
professionals working in ecology, conservation, fisheries science, or
environmental research who want to apply isotope mixing models confidently
and rigorously.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/stable-isotope-mixing-models-using-siber-siar-mixsiar-simm12/

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Learn how to build interactive data applications using R Shiny in our live online course Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01).
messages.subject_clean: Learn how to build interactive data applications using R Shiny in our live online course Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01).
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to build interactive data applications using *R Shiny* in our
live online course *Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/interactive-data-applications-with-shiny-shid01/

This practical training teaches you how to develop, deploy, and customise
interactive web applications directly from R. Whether you?re creating
dashboards for collaborators, visualising complex datasets, or building
tools for reproducible reporting, this course provides structured, hands-on
instruction from foundational concepts to advanced Shiny workflows.

The course covers:

   -

   Introduction to R Shiny fundamentals
   -

   Designing reactive user interfaces
   -

   Building interactive visualisations and data tools
   -

   Modular app design and organisation
   -

   Deploying applications online and version control integration
   -

   Best practices for reproducibility and performance

Delivered live online, the course offers real-time interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of your own use
cases. All participants receive course materials, example code, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 28-29 April 2026
Duration: 2 days (approximately 6 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?300

This course is ideal for researchers, data scientists, analysts, and
professionals who want practical skills in building interactive data
applications that enhance data exploration, communication, and
reproducibility.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/interactive-data-applications-with-shiny-shid01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Bayesian Modelling Using R & INLA - Dr Virgilio Gómez-Rubio
messages.subject_clean: Bayesian Modelling Using R & INLA - Dr Virgilio Gómez-Rubio
messages.body_plain:
Advance your statistical modelling skills with our live online course *Bayesian
Modelling Using R & INLA (BMIN04)*.

https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-modelling-using-r-inla-bmin04/

Instructor - Dr Virgilio G?mez-Rubio, a lead expert in the application of
INLA and the author of 'Bayesian Inference with INLA'.

This applied training provides a practical introduction to Bayesian
inference using the Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA)
framework in R. Participants will learn both conceptual foundations and
hands-on implementation of Bayesian models for complex data structures,
with workflows suitable for spatial, temporal, and hierarchical analyses.

The course covers:

   -

   Bayesian fundamentals and interpretation
   -

   Specification and fitting of Bayesian models in R using INLA
   -

   Model selection, comparison, and diagnostics
   -

   Handling spatial, temporal, and spatio-temporal data
   -

   Hierarchical and mixed-effects modelling in a Bayesian framework
   -

   Practical strategies for reproducible Bayesian workflows

Delivered live online, this course offers direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions about your own data, and
structured guidance through practical examples. All participants receive
course materials, example code, and post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 4-8 May 2026
Duration: 5 days (approximately 7 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?500

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, analysts,
and professionals interested in applying Bayesian methods to real data
using R and INLA.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-modelling-using-r-inla-bmin04/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Deep Learning for Complex Datasets with AI 3.0 – livestream seminar
messages.subject_clean: Deep Learning for Complex Datasets with AI 3.0 – livestream seminar
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Instats is offering a new 1-day seminar Deep Learning for Complex Datasets with AI 3.0 livestreaming March 25, led by Nikolay Oskolkov (Group Leader (PI) at LIOS). This seminar explores deep learning techniques for analyzing complex datasets, offering powerful methods to uncover intricate patterns and hidden structures within your ecological and environmental data. Participants will learn to apply advanced neural network architectures, including convolutional and recurrent networks, to model time-series, spatial, and high-dimensional data relevant to vegetation, soil, and hydrology studies. The practical skills gained using R, Python, TensorFlow, and AI tools like ChatGPT will directly enhance your ability to develop sophisticated analytical workflows and extract novel insights from your research.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/deep-learning-for-complex-datasets-with-4) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms
messages.subject_clean: Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms
messages.body_plain:
Develop advanced Bayesian modelling skills in our live online course *Bayesian
Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms (BMSB01)*.

This hands-on workshop provides a comprehensive introduction to Bayesian
data analysis using *Stan* via the *brms*package in R. Participants learn
both the theoretical foundations of Bayesian inference and practical
workflows for fitting and interpreting Bayesian models with real datasets.

The course covers the full Bayesian modelling workflow, including how to
define priors, fit models, evaluate convergence, and interpret posterior
distributions.

*The course covers:*

   -

   Foundations of Bayesian reasoning and statistical inference
   -

   Fitting Bayesian regression models in R using the *brms* interface to
   Stan
   -

   Understanding likelihoods, priors, and posterior distributions
   -

   Model comparison and Bayesian diagnostics
   -

   Interpreting MCMC output (trace plots, R-hat, effective sample size)
   -

   Extending models to generalized linear and hierarchical models

Through practical coding exercises, participants gain experience
implementing Bayesian models and interpreting results in a modern
statistical workflow.

Delivered live online, the course allows direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of your own
modelling challenges. All participants receive course materials, example
code, and post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 5?7 May 2026
Duration: 3 days (approximately 6 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?400

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, and
analysts who want to apply Bayesian methods to real datasets using R and
Stan.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-statistical-modelling-with-stan-and-brms-bmsb01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Movement Ecology (the Analysis of Movement Data)
messages.subject_clean: Movement Ecology (the Analysis of Movement Data)
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to analyse animal tracking data in our live online course *Movement
Ecology (the Analysis of Movement Data) (MOVE09)*.

https://prstats.org/course/movement-ecology-the-analysis-of-movement-data-move09/

Modern ecological research increasingly relies on movement datasets
collected from technologies such as GPS tags, biologgers, satellite
telemetry, and other tracking devices. This course provides a structured
introduction to the *movement ecology framework* and practical methods for
analysing movement data using R.

Participants will learn how to explore movement trajectories, quantify
spatial behaviour, and investigate how animals interact with their
environment.

*The course covers:*

   -

   Core concepts in movement ecology and tracking technologies
   -

   Preparing and exploring movement datasets
   -

   Home-range estimation and spatial movement metrics
   -

   Interaction metrics and behavioural interpretation
   -

   Resource selection and step-selection modelling
   -

   Reproducible workflows for analysing movement data in R

Through practical exercises and real examples, participants will gain
hands-on experience analysing movement trajectories and interpreting
ecological patterns from tracking data.

Delivered live online, the course allows direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of real research
applications. All participants receive course materials, example code, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 11?15 May 2026
Duration: 5 days (approximately 8 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?480

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, ecological
consultants, and environmental professionals working with animal tracking
or spatial movement data.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/movement-ecology-the-analysis-of-movement-data-move09/

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] (Online course) Handling Missing Data in R -22–24 April
messages.subject_clean: (Online course) Handling Missing Data in R -22–24 April
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
In our upcoming online course ?Handling Missing Data in R? (22?24 April ), we will explore how to diagnose missing data patterns and select appropriate analytical strategies. Participants will learn how to distinguish between different missingness mechanisms (MCAR, MAR, MNAR), visualise and assess missing data structures, and implement both traditional and modern imputation methods in R, including multiple imputation and iterative algorithms.
 
 
Through practical examples and hands-on exercises, the course will provide a clear framework to help researchers choose and justify the most appropriate approach for their specific datasets and research goals.
 
More information and registration details are available here: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/missing-data-with-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/missing-data-with-r/ ) 
Best regards,
Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Evidence Synthesis and Meta-analysis in R | Online Course, 11–14 May
messages.subject_clean: Evidence Synthesis and Meta-analysis in R | Online Course, 11–14 May
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
You can still join our 4-day online course ?Meta-analysis in R? (11?14 May) to learn how to conduct rigorous and reproducible evidence synthesis in ecology and evolution.
 
In this hands-on course, participants will learn how to design and perform systematic reviews and meta-analyses, from question formulation and literature searching to effect size calculation, meta-analytic modelling, and interpretation of results. The course also covers multilevel meta-analysis, meta-regression, heterogeneity, publication bias, and data visualization, using R and widely used packages such as metafor and orchaRd.
 
The program combines lectures and practical exercises with real datasets, providing participants with the skills needed to apply evidence synthesis methods to their own research projects.
 
If you are interested in learning how to integrate findings across studies and perform robust meta-analyses in R, this course will provide the practical tools to get started: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/metain-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/metain-r/ ) 
 
Best regards,
 
Carlo

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Spatial Statistics in R for Ecological Data
messages.subject_clean: Spatial Statistics in R for Ecological Data
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Discover how to enhance your ecological research by mastering spatial data analysis techniques. Spatial Statistics in R led by Nick Bearman (University College London and Geospatial Training Solutions) is now available as an on-demand streaming seminar. This workshop introduces spatial statistics using R, a vital skill for understanding patterns and relationships in geographically referenced data. Ecologists and environmental scientists can apply these techniques to analyze spatial dependencies in vegetation, soil, or hydrological data, enriching their research outcomes with nuanced spatial insights. Participants will learn to use R and GeoDa for exploratory spatial data analysis, calculate Moran's I and LISA, and perform spatial regressions, enabling more robust analysis of their own ecological datasets.

Access this seminar (https://instats.org/seminar/spatial-statistics-in-r-1) whenever convenient and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] (Online course) Metabarcoding in Microbial Ecology (16–20 March)
messages.subject_clean: (Online course) Metabarcoding in Microbial Ecology (16–20 March)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We would like to let you know that only 2 seats remain available for the upcoming Physalia online course ?Metabarcoding in Microbial Ecology?, taking place 16?20 March.
 
This course provides a comprehensive introduction to microbial metabarcoding, guiding participants through the full workflow of a metabarcoding study?from raw sequencing data processing to multivariate statistical analysis and data visualization.
 
Importantly, all hands-on sessions will be conducted using QIIME2 and R.
 
Through a combination of lectures and practical hands-on sessions, participants will learn how to:

Process next-generation sequencing reads from metabarcoding studies

Analyze microbial community composition and diversity

Apply statistical approaches for ecological interpretation

Visualize and interpret metabarcoding results
The course is designed for researchers and students interested in microbial ecology, biodiversity, and community analysis. 
 
 
 
More information and registration are available on our website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course30/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course30/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] AI for Systematic Reviews – last chance
messages.subject_clean: AI for Systematic Reviews – last chance
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance to join our 2-day seminar AI for Systematic Reviews livestreaming March 11?12 with Hadi Fariborzi (Bissett School of Business, Mount Royal University, Calgary). This seminar offers a rigorous framework for evidence synthesis, leveraging AI and R to manage the increasing volume of ecological and environmental literature. Participants will learn to integrate AI-assisted screening and codebook-driven data extraction, enabling the creation of robust, analysis-ready datasets for their own ecological meta-analyses and syntheses in R. These advanced techniques, demonstrated with parallel R workflows, will enhance the scale, reproducibility, and scientific validity of their research on vegetation, soils, or hydrology.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/ai-for-systematic-reviews) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Mechanistic Species Distribution Modelling: Ecological Niche Modelling with NicheMapR
messages.subject_clean: Mechanistic Species Distribution Modelling: Ecological Niche Modelling with NicheMapR
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to build *mechanistic species distribution models* in our live
online course *Mechanistic Species Distribution Modelling: Ecological Niche
Modelling with NicheMapR (MSDM01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/mechanistic-species-distribution-modelling-ecological-niche-modelling-with-nichemapr-msdm01/

Mechanistic species distribution models (SDMs) provide a powerful
alternative to traditional correlative approaches by incorporating
*physiological
processes, energetics, and environmental constraints* that determine where
species can persist. These approaches allow researchers to explore how
organisms respond to environmental conditions and predict species
distributions under current and future climate scenarios. ()

This applied training introduces the theoretical foundations of mechanistic
ecological niche modelling and provides practical guidance on implementing
models in *R using the NicheMapR framework*.

*The course covers:*

   -

   Foundations of ecological niche theory and mechanistic SDMs
   -

   Differences between mechanistic and correlative modelling approaches
   -

   Incorporating physiology, energetics, and environmental constraints into
   models
   -

   Using the *NicheMapR* package to simulate organism?environment
   interactions
   -

   Building mechanistic niche models and interpreting outputs
   -

   Applications to climate change, conservation, and biodiversity
   forecasting

Through hands-on exercises and real examples, participants will gain
practical experience constructing and analysing mechanistic niche models
within reproducible R workflows.

Delivered live online, the course allows direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of real research
applications. All participants receive course materials, example code, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 7 April 2026
Duration: 1 days (approximately 4.5 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?150

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, and
professionals working in ecology, conservation, biodiversity modelling, or
climate-change research who want to develop mechanistic approaches to
predicting species distributions.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/mechanistic-species-distribution-modelling-ecological-niche-modelling-with-nichemapr-msdm01/

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

Modelling with NicheMapR (MSDM01)*.

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Interactive Maps with tmap & Shiny
messages.subject_clean: Interactive Maps with tmap & Shiny
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to build powerful *interactive maps and web mapping applications
in R* in our live online course *Interactive Maps with tmap & Shiny
(IMTS01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/interactive-maps-with-tmap-shiny-imts01/

*Instructor* - Dr. Martijn Tennekes (author of the tmap package)

Interactive maps are increasingly used for exploring spatial data,
communicating results, and building decision-support tools. This course
provides practical training in creating interactive maps using the
*tmap* package
and integrating them into *Shiny applications*, allowing you to turn
spatial analyses into dynamic web-based tools. Interactive mapping
frameworks such as tmap enable users to visualise spatial patterns and
explore datasets through map layers, zooming, and dynamic interaction.

Participants will learn how to build interactive mapping workflows that can
be deployed as dashboards, data exploration tools, or web applications.

*The course covers:*

   -

   Foundations of interactive spatial data visualisation in R
   -

   Creating interactive maps using the *tmap* package
   -

   Working with spatial data using packages such as *sf*
   -

   Building user interfaces and reactive elements with *Shiny*
   -

   Integrating maps into interactive dashboards
   -

   Exporting and deploying web-based mapping applications

Through hands-on exercises and real datasets, participants will gain
practical experience designing interactive maps and building simple Shiny
applications for spatial data exploration and communication.

Delivered live online, the course offers direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of real research
or data visualisation applications. All participants receive course
materials, example code, and post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 27-29 May 2026
Duration: 3 days (approximately 5 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?350

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, analysts,
and professionals who want to develop practical skills in building
interactive maps and spatial dashboards in R.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/interactive-maps-with-tmap-shiny-imts01/

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Advanced Static Cartography in R (ASCR01)
messages.subject_clean: Advanced Static Cartography in R (ASCR01)
messages.body_plain:
Create *high-quality, publication-ready maps* in R with our live online
course *Advanced Static Cartography in R (ASCR01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/advanced-static-cartography-in-r-ascr01/

*Instructor* - Dr. Martijn Tennekes (author of the tmap package)

Effective maps are essential for communicating spatial patterns, supporting
analysis, and presenting results in research and policy contexts. This
course focuses on advanced cartographic techniques for producing *clear,
visually compelling static maps* using R?s spatial data ecosystem and
modern cartography tools. R is widely used for spatial data analysis and
visualisation because it provides a reproducible and flexible environment
supported by a large ecosystem of spatial packages.

Participants will learn how to apply professional cartographic principles
to improve map clarity, aesthetics, and interpretability, while developing
reproducible workflows for spatial visualisation.

*The course covers:*

   -

   Principles of effective cartographic design
   -

   Creating high-quality static maps in R
   -

   Working with spatial data using packages such as *sf*, *terra*, and
   related tools
   -

   Designing clear map layouts, legends, and annotations
   -

   Choosing effective colour schemes and visual hierarchies
   -

   Exporting publication-quality maps for reports and scientific
   publications

Through hands-on exercises and real datasets, participants will gain
practical experience designing clear and visually effective maps suitable
for research papers, reports, and presentations.

Delivered live online, the course offers direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of real mapping
and visualisation challenges. All participants receive course materials,
example code, and post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 3-5 June 2026
Duration: 3 days (approximately 5 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?350

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, analysts,
and professionals who work with spatial data and want to produce
high-quality maps using reproducible workflows in R.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/advanced-static-cartography-in-r-ascr01/

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Species Distribution Modelling (SDMs) and Ecological Niche Modelling (ENMs) (SDMR07)
messages.subject_clean: Species Distribution Modelling (SDMs) and Ecological Niche Modelling (ENMs) (SDMR07)
messages.body_plain:
Develop practical skills in predicting species distributions and
environmental niches in our live online course *Species Distribution
Modelling (SDMs) and Ecological Niche Modelling (ENMs) (SDMR07)*.

https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-sdms-and-ecological-niche-modelling-enms-sdmr07/

Species distribution models are widely used in ecology, conservation, and
environmental science to understand how environmental factors shape
species? geographic ranges and to predict how distributions may change
under different environmental scenarios. ()

This intensive course provides a comprehensive introduction to SDM and ENM
techniques using *R*, combining theoretical foundations with hands-on
modelling exercises.

Participants will learn how to build, evaluate, and interpret models that
link species occurrence data with environmental variables to generate
predictions of habitat suitability and geographic distributions.

*The course covers:*

   -

   Foundations of species distribution and ecological niche modelling
   -

   Preparing occurrence and environmental datasets for modelling
   -

   Building models using methods such as *Maxent, Bioclim, and logistic
   regression*
   -

   Using R packages such as *dismo*, *biomod2*, and *ecospat* for ENM
   workflows
   -

   Model evaluation, validation, and comparison
   -

   Mapping and interpreting predicted species distributions

Through practical exercises and real datasets, participants will gain
experience implementing SDM and ENM workflows and interpreting results
within a reproducible R-based framework.

Delivered live online, the course offers direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of real research
applications. All participants receive course materials, datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 11?15 May 2026
Duration: 5 days (approximately 8 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?480

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, ecological
consultants, and environmental professionals interested in modelling
species distributions and ecological niches using modern analytical tools.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-sdms-and-ecological-niche-modelling-enms-sdmr07/

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

environmental niches in our live online course *Species Distribution
Modelling (SDMs) and Ecological Niche Modelling (ENMs) (SDMR07)*.

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Online course: Causal Inference with AI and ML
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: Causal Inference with AI and ML
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - We are pleased to announce an upcoming seminar that will explore advanced analytical methods. Causal Inference with AI and Machine Learning is a 2-day seminar livestreaming March 12?13 with Jason Anastasopoulos (School of Public and International Affairs, University of Georgia). This seminar equips researchers with robust methods to move beyond correlation, enabling them to precisely identify cause-and-effect relationships within complex ecological datasets. Participants will learn to implement advanced machine learning and AI techniques in R, enhancing their ability to design credible studies and interpret heterogeneous treatment effects in environmental systems. These skills, including LLM-assisted diagnostics, can be applied to stress-test assumptions in ecological models and generate clear explanations for observed environmental changes.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/causal-inference-with-ai-and-machine-lea) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online Course: GLLVMs – Advanced Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities in R
messages.subject_clean: Online Course: GLLVMs – Advanced Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We are excited to announce the upcoming Physalia online course:
?GLLVMs: Advanced Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities in R?
 
 Dates: 7?10 July 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/gllvm/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/gllvm/ ) 
 
 
Multivariate analysis of ecological communities has traditionally relied on distance-based methods such as CCA, NMDS, or PCoA. Over the last decade, Generalized Linear Latent Variable Models (GLLVMs) have emerged as a flexible and unified framework for model-based ordination and for jointly analysing species occurrence patterns, similar to Joint Species Distribution Models (JSDMs).
 
This course combines conceptual lectures and hands-on labs in R, covering:

Multispecies GL(M)Ms and model-based ordination

Joint Species Distribution Models

Diagnostics, model selection, and visualization of results

Applying models to your own ecological datasets
 
Participants will leave the course with practical experience applying GLLVMs to complex ecological data and the ability to interpret, visualise, and communicate results effectively.
 
 
Best regards, Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Species Distribution & Ecological Niche Modelling Training Series
messages.subject_clean: Species Distribution & Ecological Niche Modelling Training Series
messages.body_plain:
Species Distribution & Ecological Niche Modelling Training Series

Understanding and predicting where species occur is central to modern
ecology, conservation planning, and environmental management. Our training
series in Species Distribution Modelling (SDMs) and Ecological Niche
Modelling (ENMs) provides practical, hands-on instruction in the tools and
methods used to analyse species?environment relationships and forecast
species distributions.

Explore the individual courses below to find the training most suited to
your research or professional needs.

Mechanistic Species Distribution Modelling / Ecological Niche Modelling
with NicheMapR (MSDM01)
Learn mechanistic species distribution and ecological niche modelling with
NicheMapR in R. Hands-on live online course with microclimate modelling.
https://prstats.org/course/mechanistic-species-distribution-modelling-ecological-niche-modelling-with-nichemapr-msdm01/

Model Validation for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling
(MVSD01)
Learn how to validate species distribution and ecological niche models.
https://prstats.org/course/model-validation-for-species-distribution-and-ecological-niche-modelling-mvsd01/

Standard modelling procedure for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche
Modelling (SDMS01)
Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling is an applied R course
teaching standard workflows for building, evaluating, and interpreting SDMs.
https://prstats.org/course/standard-modelling-procedure-for-species-distribution-and-ecological-niche-modelling-sdms01/

Species Distribution Modelling (SDMs) and Ecological Niche Modelling (ENMs)
(SDMR07)
Learn ENM and SDM modelling in R. Apply tools like Maxent and Biomod2 to
predict species distributions and environmental niches.
https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-sdms-and-ecological-niche-modelling-enms-sdmr07/

Species Distribution Modelling With Bayesian Statistics (SDMB08)
Model species distributions using BART in R. Covers uncertainty, variable
selection, and full Bayesian workflow.
https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-with-bayesian-statistics-sdmb08/

These courses cover the full modelling workflow, from building and
interpreting models to validating predictions and applying advanced
statistical and mechanistic approaches. Participants will learn how to use
widely adopted tools in R, work with real ecological datasets, and apply
best practices for producing robust and reproducible models.

The series includes courses covering:
Standard modelling workflows for building and interpreting SDMs
Advanced modelling techniques including Bayesian approaches
Mechanistic modelling frameworks that incorporate physiological processes
Model validation and evaluation to ensure reliable predictions
Applied modelling tools such as Maxent, Biomod2, BART, and NicheMapR

Whether you are new to species distribution modelling or looking to expand
your analytical toolkit, these courses provide a structured pathway to
developing strong practical skills in ecological niche modelling.

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
messages.body_snippet:

Understanding and predicting where species occur is central to modern
ecology, conservation planning, and environmental management. O

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Online course: R Programming for Statistics and Machine Learning
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: R Programming for Statistics and Machine Learning
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - This seminar offers a comprehensive exploration of R programming for statistical and machine learning applications. R Programming for Statistics and Machine Learning is a 2-day seminar livestreaming March 27 with Nikolay Oskolkov (Group Leader (PI) at LIOS). This seminar explores R programming as an integrated toolkit for contemporary statistical inference and machine learning, emphasizing both theoretical foundations and practical implementation for reproducible research. Participants will learn to implement methods like penalised regression (LASSO), partial least squares, discriminant analysis, dimensionality reduction (PCA, tSNE, UMAP), and various clustering techniques in R. These advanced R programming skills are directly applicable for ecologists and environmental scientists analyzing complex, high-dimensional data, enabling robust model selection, feature engineering, and transparent reporting in their own research.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/r-programming-for-statistics-and-machine) to reserve your place before it starts, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Analysing Biodiversity through Time and Space Using R
messages.subject_clean: Analysing Biodiversity through Time and Space Using R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
registrations are now open for the 3rd edition of the upcoming online course, ?Analysing Biodiversity through Time and Space Using R?, taking place from 13th to 17th July.
 
This course provides a comprehensive, hands-on guide to constructing reproducible workflows for biodiversity data acquisition, cleaning, and analysis in R. The workflows will be applicable to both palaeontological and modern datasets, enabling seamless integration for analysis across temporal and spatial scales.
 
Each day, participants will explore the processes shaping biodiversity patterns, including latitudinal gradients, hotspots, and mass extinctions, with discussions providing context for practical exercises. These exercises focus on identifying errors and sampling artefacts in biodiversity datasets, estimating diversity and diversification rates, and visualising data to understand its limitations.
 
By the end of the course, participants will have a clear understanding of occurrence data, GIS applications, chronostratigraphy, palaeogeographic models, diversity metrics, and methods to account for sampling variation. You will also learn to bridge modern and fossil biodiversity analyses, quantify ecological diversity, and test hypotheses on biodiversity patterns.
 
The course is structured with lectures and discussions from 15:00 to 18:00 Berlin time, complemented by self-paced practicals of approximately four hours daily. A pre-course optional refresher on base R is available for those who wish to strengthen their programming foundation.
 
For more information, please visit: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/biodiversity-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/biodiversity-in-r/ )
 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 
 
 

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Online course: RNA-seq FASTQ to counts workflow
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: RNA-seq FASTQ to counts workflow
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - Learn essential skills for processing RNA-seq data, from raw files to publication-ready count matrices. RNA-seq: From FASTQ to Counts is a 2-day seminar livestreaming March 23 and 25 with Estefania Mancini (Josep Carreras Leukaemia Research Institute). The seminar focuses on reproducible RNA-seq data processing using nf-core Nextflow pipelines, from raw FASTQ files to read count matrices. Ecologists and environmental scientists can apply these robust methods to analyze gene expression in environmental samples, study stress responses in organisms, or characterize microbial communities, generating high-quality data for downstream R-based statistical analysis. You will gain practical skills to implement portable workflows, interpret quality control outputs, and produce publication-ready count matrices, enhancing the rigor and reproducibility of your genomic research.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/rna-seq-from-fastq-to-counts) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online course: Bregman Projection for Calibration
messages.subject_clean: Online course: Bregman Projection for Calibration
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

We invite you to a seminar exploring advanced statistical methods for improving data analysis. Bregman Projection for Calibration Estimation (Free Seminar) is a 2-day seminar livestreaming April 2, led by Jae-Kwang Kim (Department of Statistics, Iowa State University). This seminar introduces calibration weighting as a Bregman projection, offering a unified framework for integrating auxiliary information and mitigating selection bias in survey estimation. Researchers in ecology and environmental science often encounter complex survey data and need robust methods to account for non-random sampling or incorporate diverse environmental covariates. Understanding this framework will equip participants to enhance the accuracy and robustness of their ecological and environmental data analyses, particularly when dealing with high-dimensional data or aiming for debiased predictions.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/bregman-projection-for-calibration-estim) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject_clean: (Online course) Species Distribution Modeling with BART (1–3 July)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
Do you already have some experience with species distribution modeling and want to learn a modern, powerful method to take your analyses further?
 
Join our online course, Species Distribution Modeling with BART (1?3 July) and gain hands-on experience with:

Building and evaluating BART models in R for species occurrence

Selecting informative predictors using embarcadero and dbarts

Visualizing and interpreting effects and interactions in your models

Projecting species distributions into new regions or times
Each day combines live sessions with guided practical exercises and live support to make sure you can
 
If interested, check it out: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/barts/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/barts/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online course: Advanced R for Spatial Analysis
messages.subject_clean: Online course: Advanced R for Spatial Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Discover advanced techniques for spatial data analysis that can elevate your research impact. Advanced R for Spatial Analysis led by Nick Bearman (University College London and Geospatial Training Solutions) is now available as an on-demand streaming seminar. This seminar provides advanced R skills for spatial analysis, enabling ecological and environmental researchers to precisely analyze complex spatial data relevant to vegetation, soils, and hydrology. Participants will learn to perform spatial joins, buffering, and point-in-polygon operations, which are crucial for integrating diverse ecological datasets and making informed spatial decisions in their studies. Mastering these techniques will empower researchers to transform raw spatial data into actionable insights, enhancing the rigor and impact of their work in environmental science.

Access this seminar (https://instats.org/seminar/advanced-r-for-spatial-analysis-1) at your own pace and please share this with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Online course: Agentic Data Analytics for Research Workflows
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Online course: Agentic Data Analytics for Research Workflows
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - Discover how to revolutionize your research processes by leveraging advanced AI for data analysis. Agentic Data Analytics: Automating Research Workflows is a 2-day seminar livestreaming March 24 and 27 with Peter Gruber (Faculty of Economics, USI Universit? della Svizzera Italiana). Agentic AI offers a powerful new approach to automate entire scientific workflows, from data cleaning to report generation, which can significantly streamline research in ecology and environmental science. This seminar introduces Google Antigravity, a tool that enables researchers to use advanced AI models like Gemini Pro and Claude Opus to perform complex data analytics tasks in R or Python locally, ensuring data privacy. By mastering these agentic workflows, R-sig-ecology members can apply these skills to efficiently wrangle ecological datasets, conduct statistical analyses, and generate publication-ready visualizations and reports for their specific research questions.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/agentic-data-analytics-automating-resear) to register now, and we'd appreciate you sharing this with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online course:  Stable Isotope Ecology with R (18–21 May)
messages.subject_clean: Online course:  Stable Isotope Ecology with R (18–21 May)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
I?d like to share with you one of our upcoming courses: Applied Stable Isotope Ecology with R ? from data to decisions, which will run online from 18?21 May.
 
Course website: https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/stable-isotope-ecology/
 
The idea behind this course is to give a clear and practical introduction to stable isotope ecology, starting from the basics and moving towards real data analysis in R. We?ll cover concepts such as isotope fractionation, baselines, tissue turnover, and isotopic niches, and show how these can be used to answer ecological and environmental questions.
 
A big focus of the course is on linking theory to practice. Each topic is paired with hands-on sessions, so participants can see how analyses are done step by step, understand the assumptions behind them, and learn how to interpret the results properly.
 
The course is open to students, researchers, and professionals working in ecology, fisheries, conservation, or environmental science. No prior experience with stable isotopes is needed, although some familiarity with R will be helpful.
 
Over the four days (14:00?18:00 Berlin time), we will cover:
Foundations of stable isotope ecology and data visualisation
Mixing models (SIMMR, CoSIMMR, MixSIAR)
Estimating trophic position
Isotopic niches and food web metrics
 
If this sounds interesting, feel free to get in touch for more details or registration information.
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Multivariate data analysis with R and vegan course
messages.subject_clean: Multivariate data analysis with R and vegan course
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
registration is still open for our Multivariate Data Analysis with R and vegan course, held online from 4?7 May. This hands-on course provides a practical introduction to multivariate statistical analysis in R, with a focus on the widely used vegan package.
 
Participants will gain experience with:
Unconstrained and constrained ordination methods (PCA, CCA, RDA, PCoA, NMDS)
Between-group tests such as PERMANOVA and PERMDISP
Restricted permutation tests and experimental design considerations
Visualising and interpreting multivariate data in R
Applying methods to high-throughput datasets such as metabarcoding and metagenomics
 A basic familiarity with R and RStudio is required.
 
Sessions combine lectures, practical exercises, and discussions, providing a strong foundation to analyse and interpret complex multivariate data sets.
 
More information and registration: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ ) 
 
Best regards, Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D
